questions.question: What are the requirements of an ideal major connector? Name the types.
questions.answer:
**Requirements of ideal major connector**:

(1) **Rigidity** - Must be rigid to prevent flexure which damages abutments and denture base,

(2) **Adequate bulk** - Sufficient cross-sectional thickness for rigidity without excessive tissue coverage,

(3) **Location** - Should be placed to avoid tissue interference and impingement,

(4) **Tissue relief** - Adequate relief over soft tissues (0.3-0.5mm for maxilla, 0.5-1.0mm for mandible),

(5) **Tissue coverage** - Minimum necessary coverage to avoid tongue/food interference,

(6) **Comfort** - Smooth, polished surfaces, proper location,

(7) **Border extension** - Should not impinge on moving tissues (frenum, floor of mouth). **Types - Maxillary**:

(1) **Single palatal strap/bar** (anteroposterior palatal bar),

(2) **Double palatal bar** (anterior-posterior),

(3) **Complete/full palatal plate**,

(4) **Anteroposterior palatal strap** (combined anterior and posterior palatal bar),

(5) **U-shaped palatal connector**,

(6) **Horseshoe connector**. **Types - Mandibular**:

(1) **Lingual bar** (most common, 6-8mm high, 3mm from gingival margin),

(2) **Lingual plate** (when lingual bar not feasible),

(3) **Labial bar** (rare, in severe lingual undercuts),

(4) **Continuous clasp** (Kennedy bar).

questions.question: Compare circumferential clasp and bar clasp (I-bar). When is each indicated?
questions.answer:
**Circumferential Clasp (C-clasp/Aker's clasp)**: Originates **above** the height of contour, approaches undercut from **occlusal direction**, encircles more than **180 degrees** of tooth. **Advantages**: Good retention and reciprocation, stable, simple design. **Disadvantages**: More visible, requires greater tooth coverage. **Indications**: Posterior teeth, when esthetics not critical, adequate facial undercut below survey line. **Bar Clasp (I-bar/Roach clasp)**: Originates **below** the height of contour (from denture base), approaches undercut from **gingival direction**, engages **buccal/facial** undercut only. **Advantages**: Better esthetics (approaches from gingival), more flexible, less tooth coverage, allows tissue-ward movement of distal extension base. **Disadvantages**: More difficult to fabricate, requires adequate undercut, less stable. **Indications**:

(1) **Anterior teeth** where esthetics is important,

(2) **Premolars** with good facial undercut,

(3) **Distal extension bases** (Class I, II) - allows vertical movement without stressing abutment,

(4) Teeth with minimal **mesial-distal undercut**. **Common features**: Both have retentive arm, reciprocal arm, and rest. **RPI system** (Rest, Proximal plate, I-bar) commonly used for distal extension RPDs for optimal stress distribution.

questions.question: What are stress breakers in RPD? What are the advantages and disadvantages?
questions.answer:
**Stress breakers** (also called stress equalizers or shock absorbers) are mechanical devices incorporated between the **retainer/clasp** and the **denture base** in distal extension RPDs (Kennedy Class I and II) to allow **independent movement** of the extension base without transmitting stress to abutment teeth. **Types**:

(1) **Hinge-type** (Dalbo, Ceka),

(2) **Spring-loaded** devices,

(3) **Split major connector**. **Rationale**: Distal extension base moves tissue-ward under function (tissues compress); rigid attachment transmits this movement as rotational force on abutments causing mobility. Stress breakers allow base to move independently. **Advantages**:

(1) **Reduced stress** on abutment teeth,

(2) Allows tissue-ward movement of extension base,

(3) May preserve abutments longer in compromised periodontium. **Disadvantages**:

(1) **Increased complexity** and cost,

(2) **Maintenance problems** - wear, breakage of mechanical parts,

(3) **Food impaction** at hinge area,

(4) **Loss of indirect retention**,

(5) **Accelerated ridge resorption** due to concentrated stress on edentulous ridge,

(6) Difficulty in achieving proper **stress distribution**. **Modern approach**: Most prosthodontists prefer **proper RPD design** (RPI system, adequate tissue coverage, accurate fit, proper rest placement) over stress breakers for managing stress in distal extension RPDs.

questions.question: What is altered cast technique? When is it indicated?
questions.answer:
**Altered cast technique** (also called functional relining or corrected cast technique) is a method of making a **functional impression** of the **edentulous ridge** in distal extension RPDs (Kennedy Class I and II) while the framework is **fully seated on the abutment teeth**. **Rationale**: Master cast is made with tissues at rest (preliminary impression); under function, soft tissues compress. Altered cast technique records tissues under functional load for better support. **Procedure**:

(1) Fabricate metal framework on master cast,

(2) Try-in framework, ensure proper fit on abutments,

(3) **Remove denture base portion** of edentulous area from master cast,

(4) Apply **impression material** (ZnOE paste, elastomer) to framework in edentulous area,

(5) Seat framework in mouth with **rests fully seated**,

(6) Ask patient to make **functional movements** while material sets,

(7) Remove impression with framework,

(8) **Pour new cast** in impression for edentulous area only (abutment portion unchanged),

(9) Process denture base on this altered cast. **Indications**:

(1) **Distal extension RPDs** (Class I, II),

(2) **Resorbed ridges** with soft, compressible tissues,

(3) When **optimal support** from edentulous ridge needed. **Advantage**: Better fit of denture base under functional load, improved retention and stability. **Alternative**: Functional impression using altered cast impression tray.

questions.question: What instructions should be given to a patient regarding RPD maintenance?
questions.answer:
**RPD maintenance instructions**: **Daily Care**:

(1) **Remove and clean RPD after every meal** using soft brush and mild soap/denture cleaner,

(2) Clean **abutment teeth thoroughly** especially around clasps and rest seats,

(3) **Rinse mouth** after removing RPD,

(4) Use **interdental brushes** or floss around abutments. **Night-time**:

(5) **Remove RPD at night** to allow tissue rest, soak in water or denture cleanser,

(6) Never use **hot water** (causes warpage). **Oral Hygiene**:

(7) Brush remaining teeth **at least twice daily** with fluoride toothpaste,

(8) Special attention to **abutment teeth** which bear maximum stress,

(9) Use **mouth rinse** if recommended. **Handling**:

(10) Handle RPD over **folded towel or water basin** to prevent breakage if dropped,

(11) **Never bend clasps** or adjust RPD yourself. **Diet**:

(12) Cut food into small pieces, **chew bilaterally**,

(13) Avoid **very hard/sticky foods** initially. **Follow-up**:

(14) Return for **regular check-ups** every 6 months,

(15) Report immediately if: **pain, looseness, fractured clasp, or tooth mobility**. **Warnings**: Never sleep with RPD continuously (causes tissue damage), never use abrasive cleaners, never attempt self-repair.

questions.question: What is an overdenture? What are its advantages and disadvantages?
questions.answer:
An **overdenture** (also called **overlay denture** or **superimposed prosthesis**) is a **removable complete or partial denture** that covers and rests on **one or more remaining natural teeth, tooth roots, or dental implants**. The retained structures are modified - natural teeth are endodontically treated, reduced to gingival level, and often capped with metal copings. **Types**:

(1) **Tooth-supported overdenture**,

(2) **Implant-supported overdenture**. **Advantages**:

(1) **Preserves proprioception** - Retained periodontal ligament provides sensory feedback for better neuromuscular control,

(2) **Maintains alveolar bone** - Retaining roots **reduces/delays ridge resorption** significantly compared to complete extraction,

(3) **Improved retention and stability** - Better than conventional complete dentures, especially lower denture,

(4) **Psychological benefit** - Patient retains 'some natural teeth', improves acceptance,

(5) **Better masticatory efficiency** - Increased biting force and chewing ability compared to conventional dentures,

(6) **Economical** - Less expensive than multiple implants,

(7) **Reversible** - If abutments fail, can be converted to complete denture. **Disadvantages**:

(1) **Requires good oral hygiene** - Retained roots/teeth prone to caries and periodontal disease,

(2) **Maintenance** - Regular follow-up needed, professional cleaning,

(3) **Risk of abutment failure** - Root caries, root fracture, periodontal disease,

(4) **Increased prosthesis thickness** - Tooth/root coverage requires space, may affect phonetics. **Common abutments**: **Canines** (longest roots, favorable position) and **premolars**. **Attachments**: Precision attachments (ball, bar, magnet) can be used for better retention.

questions.question: What is an immediate denture? What are its advantages and disadvantages?
questions.answer:
An **immediate denture** (also called **transitional denture** or **temporary denture**) is a **complete or partial denture** fabricated **before extraction** of remaining teeth and **inserted immediately** after extraction in the **same appointment**. Patient is never without teeth. **Procedure**:

(1) Make preliminary impressions with teeth present,

(2) Extract **posterior teeth** 8-12 weeks before to allow healing,

(3) Make **final impressions** - record edentulous posterior areas and remaining anterior teeth,

(4) Fabricate denture - **anterior teeth extracted on cast**, replaced with denture teeth,

(5) **Extract anterior teeth** and **insert denture immediately**. **Advantages**:

(1) **No edentulous period** - Patient never without teeth, maintains appearance and confidence,

(2) **Psychological benefit** - Less traumatic transition from natural teeth to dentures,

(3) **Maintains vertical dimension** - Prevents over-closure and muscle adaptation to edentulous state,

(4) **Acts as surgical dressing** - Protects extraction sites, controls bleeding, reduces post-op pain,

(5) **Preserves jaw relations** - Easier to duplicate pre-extraction appearance,

(6) **Preserves speech and function** - Minimal adjustment period for phonetics. **Disadvantages**:

(1) **No try-in** possible - Cannot verify esthetics, occlusion before insertion,

(2) **Extensive resorption** - Significant ridge changes occur in first 3-6 months,

(3) **Multiple adjustments** required - Frequent tissue conditioner applications, relining needed,

(4) **Difficult retention initially** - Edematous tissues, surgical sites still healing,

(5) **Higher cost** - Requires relining/rebasing after healing,

(6) **Technique-sensitive** - Requires careful planning. **Post-insertion**: Tissue conditioner or soft liner applied, **permanent reline** after 3-6 months when tissues stabilized.

questions.question: What is cross-arch tooth arrangement? When is it indicated in complete dentures?
questions.answer:
**Cross-arch arrangement** (also called **reverse articulation**) is an unconventional teeth arrangement technique where **mandibular posterior teeth are arranged on the maxillary arch** and **maxillary posterior teeth on the mandibular arch** to achieve **balanced occlusion** in patients with **Class III jaw relationships** or **unfavorable ridge relations**. **Indications**:

(1) **Class III ridge relationship** - Mandibular arch is **wider** than maxillary arch (reversed from normal),

(2) **Severely resorbed maxillary ridge** with relatively well-formed mandibular ridge resulting in unfavorable arch relationship,

(3) When conventional tooth arrangement would result in **crossbite relationship** or **edge-to-edge occlusion**,

(4) Patients with **oral submucous fibrosis** with altered jaw and ridge relationships,

(5) **Prognathic mandible** with normal maxilla. **Technique**:

(1) **Anterior teeth** usually arranged in **edge-to-edge relationship** or slight reverse overjet,

(2) **Posterior teeth** - Maxillary posterior denture teeth placed on mandibular ridge, mandibular posterior denture teeth on maxillary ridge,

(3) Achieve **cusp-fossa relationship** for balanced occlusion,

(4) May use **lingualized occlusion** for better stability. **Advantages**:

(1) Provides **stable occlusion** despite unfavorable jaw relationship,

(2) Prevents **denture instability** from conventional crossbite arrangement,

(3) Better **retention and function**. **Patient counseling**: Essential to explain unusual tooth arrangement, as patient may be concerned about appearance. **Alternative**: Surgical correction of jaw relationship before denture fabrication.

questions.question: What is combination syndrome (Kelly's syndrome)? Describe its clinical features and management.
questions.answer:
**Combination syndrome** (also called **Kelly's syndrome** or **anterior hyperplasia syndrome**) is a cluster of **pathological features** occurring when a **maxillary complete denture** opposes **mandibular anterior natural teeth** or fixed partial denture, with **missing mandibular posterior teeth** (Kennedy Class I or II). **Etiology**: Unfavorable force distribution - mandibular anterior teeth exert excessive pressure on **maxillary anterior ridge** while **maxillary posterior** areas experience reduced loading. **Clinical features (5 characteristics)**:

(1) **Loss of bone** from the **anterior maxillary ridge** - Progressive resorption due to excessive forces from mandibular anteriors,

(2) **Overgrowth/hyperplasia** of **tuberosities** - Maxillary posterior areas,

(3) **Papillary hyperplasia** of **hard palate** - Inflammatory tissue overgrowth,

(4) **Extrusion** of **mandibular anterior teeth** - Due to lack of posterior occlusal stops,

(5) **Progressive resorption** of **posterior mandibular ridge** beneath distal extension RPD bases. **Additional features**: Midline fracture of maxillary denture, poor retention of maxillary denture, loss of vertical dimension. **Management**:

(1) **Extract mandibular anterior teeth** if severely extruded/mobile and fabricate complete denture (ideal),

(2) If retaining mandibular anteriors: **(a) Surgical reduction** of tuberosities and hyperplastic tissue, **(b) Reduce/restore** mandibular anterior teeth to reduce vertical forces, **(c) Establish posterior occlusion** with RPD or implant-supported prosthesis to provide **occlusal stops**, **(d) Use resilient liner** in maxillary denture to cushion anterior forces, **(e) Regular follow-up** and tissue conditioning. **Prevention**: Avoid leaving mandibular anteriors opposing maxillary complete denture without posterior support.

questions.question: What is a single complete denture? What are the special considerations in its fabrication?
questions.answer:
A **single complete denture (SCD)** is a complete denture in **one arch** (usually **maxillary**) that opposes **natural teeth** in the opposing arch (usually mandibular). Also called **unopposed complete denture**. **Challenges**:

(1) **Disparity in support** - Natural teeth have **PDL support**, denture has **mucosal support** (compressible, less stable),

(2) **Excessive forces** - Natural teeth can generate higher biting forces that may **damage denture-bearing tissues**, cause **ridge resorption**, denture instability,

(3) **Unequal occlusal plane** - Natural teeth may be extruded, tilted, or worn,

(4) **Risk of combination syndrome** if only mandibular anteriors present. **Special considerations in fabrication**:

(1) **Modify opposing natural teeth** - **(a) Equilibrate** to remove occlusal interferences, **(b) Restore** missing teeth with FPD/RPD to establish **bilateral posterior support**, **(c) Crown/reshape** severely worn/extruded teeth to create favorable occlusal plane,

(2) **Impression** - Record tissues with **minimal pressure** (mucostatic technique) to reduce tissue displacement,

(3) **Occlusal scheme** - **(a) Create bilateral balanced occlusion** if possible, or **(b) Use lingualized occlusion** to reduce lateral forces, **(c) Reduce cusp height** of denture teeth, **(d) Widen buccolingual dimension** slightly for stability,

(4) **Occlusal adjustment** - Carefully adjust to avoid **premature contacts** and excessive forces on anterior region,

(5) **Vertical dimension** - Maintain or **slightly reduce** to minimize forces on denture-bearing area,

(6) **Material selection** - Consider **resilient denture liner** to absorb shock,

(7) **Patient education** - **Soft diet initially**, avoid hard/sticky foods, regular follow-ups for adjustment and relining. **Prognosis**: Generally **poorer** than opposing complete dentures due to force disparity.
